AP Calc BC: parametric derivative dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) = 2t/1 = 2t = 2x (matches direct differentiation). Useful when y=f(x) is hard to express but parametric form is natural (cycloid, etc.).

questions.prompt: Tangent slope dy/dx for parametric x(t), y(t).
choices: integral from a to b of sqrt((dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2) dt
questions.explanation: Parametric derivative: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) where both top and bottom are derivatives with respect to t. Comes from chain rule: dy/dt = (dy/dx)(dx/dt), solving for dy/dx. Undefined when dx/dt = 0 (vertical tangent). Example: x = cos t, y = sin t: dy/dx = cos t / (-sin t) = -cot t. AP Calc BC: 'Find tangent line to parametric curve at t = pi/4.' Compute x'(pi/4), y'(pi/4), use point-slope form. Also: second derivative d²y/dx² = (d/dt[dy/dx])/(dx/dt), tricky chain rule.

questions.prompt: Arc length formula for parametric curve from t=a to t=b.
choices: x = t - sin t, y = 1 - cos t
integral from a to b of sqrt((dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2) dt
questions.correctText: integral from a to b of sqrt((dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2) dt
questions.explanation: Arc length: L = integral_a^b sqrt((dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2) dt = integral_a^b |v(t)| dt = integral of speed. Same as distance traveled by particle from t=a to t=b. For curve given as y = f(x): L = integral sqrt(1 + (dy/dx)^2) dx (special case where parameter is x itself). AP Calc BC core formula. Example: arc length of x = cos t, y = sin t from 0 to 2pi: integral sqrt(sin^2 t + cos^2 t) dt = integral 1 dt = 2pi (matches circumference of unit circle).

questions.prompt: Parametrize cycloid traced by point on circle of radius 1 rolling along x-axis.
choices: x = t - sin t, y = 1 - cos t
questions.correctText: x = t - sin t, y = 1 - cos t
questions.explanation: Cycloid: curve traced by point on rim of rolling wheel. For unit circle rolling along x-axis starting with point at origin, after circle rotates by angle t, center has moved to (t, 1), and tracked point relative to center is (-sin t, -cos t). Absolute position: (t - sin t, 1 - cos t). Period 2pi. Famous brachistochrone curve: cycloid solves the 'fastest descent under gravity' problem (Bernoulli, 1696). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: classic parametric curve example. Arc length per arch = 8.

questions.prompt: Quantity with both magnitude and direction.
questions.explanation: Vector: A quantity with both magnitude (size) and direction. Contrast with scalar (magnitude only, e.g., temperature, mass). Notation: bold v or v with arrow on top. Components: v = <a, b> in 2D or <a, b, c> in 3D. Examples: velocity, force, displacement, acceleration. PreCalc Regents introduces vectors via component form and graphical addition. AP Calc BC and Calculus III use them extensively for parametric curves, motion in space, dot/cross products. The position vector r(t) = <x(t), y(t)> describes parametric motion.

questions.prompt: Vector representing motion of a particle = position derivative.
questions.explanation: Velocity vector v(t) = r'(t) = <x'(t), y'(t)>. Direction is tangent to path; magnitude is speed. AP Calc BC: 'A particle moves along curve x(t)=..., y(t)=... Find velocity at t=2.' Compute x'(t), y'(t), evaluate at t=2. Speed = |v(t)| = sqrt(x'^2 + y'^2). Acceleration vector a(t) = v'(t) = <x''(t), y''(t)>. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC heavily tests parametric motion problems. Distance traveled = integral of speed dt, NOT integral of velocity (which is displacement).

questions.prompt: Unit vector in direction of <3, 4>.
questions.explanation: Unit vector: u_hat = v/|v|. For v=<3,4>: |v|=5, so unit vector = <3/5, 4/5>. Magnitude of unit vector is 1 by construction: sqrt((3/5)^2+(4/5)^2) = sqrt(25/25) = 1. Standard unit vectors in 2D: i = <1,0>, j = <0,1>. Any vector <a,b> = a*i + b*j. PreCalc Regents tests unit vector construction. AP Calc BC parametric: unit tangent T(t) = r'(t)/|r'(t)| measures direction of motion. Calc III: principal unit normal N(t) for curvature.

questions.prompt: Parametric equations for line through (1, 2) with direction <3, 4>.
questions.explanation: Parametric line: For line through P_0 = (1,2) with direction vector d=<3,4>, parametric form is (x,y) = (1,2) + t*<3,4>, giving x = 1 + 3t, y = 2 + 4t. Cartesian: slope = 4/3, equation y - 2 = (4/3)(x-1). Vector form preserves direction. As t varies over reals, line is fully traced. PreCalc Regents fundamental. AP Calc BC: position-velocity-acceleration analysis with parametric motion. Calc III extends to 3D lines and planes: r(t) = r_0 + t*v.

questions.prompt: Vector projection of <4, 1> onto <2, 0>.
questions.explanation: Vector projection: proj_u(v) = ((u.v)/|u|^2) u. Here u=<2,0>, v=<4,1>: u.v = 4*2 + 1*0 = 8. |u|^2 = 4. Scalar coefficient = 8/4 = 2. proj = 2*<2,0> = <4,0>. Geometric: 'shadow' of v cast onto direction of u. Used in physics (work = F.d projection), computer graphics, statistics (least squares regression). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: motion problems where velocity vector decomposes into components along and across a road/river. Calc III: gradient projection onto direction = directional derivative.

questions.prompt: X in polar form (r, theta).
questions.explanation: X = r cos θ, y = r sin θ. r is distance from origin, θ is angle from positive x-axis. Reverse: r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2), tan θ = y/x (with quadrant check). Polar (1, pi/2): x = 1*0 = 0, y = 1*1 = 1, point (0,1). Polar (2, pi): point (-2, 0). PreCalc Regents introduces conversion. AP Calc BC tests polar curves like r = 1 + cos θ (cardioid) and r = 2 cos(3θ) (rose). Polar form is convenient for circular and radial symmetry, even when Cartesian form is awkward.

questions.prompt: Y in polar form (r, theta).
questions.explanation: Polar y-coordinate: y = r sin θ. Combined with x = r cos θ: any point (r, θ) in polar maps to (r cos θ, r sin θ) in Cartesian. Distance from origin: sqrt(x^2 + y^2) = sqrt(r^2 cos^2 + r^2 sin^2) = sqrt(r^2(cos^2 + sin^2)) = |r|. Identity recovers r. Polar form (r, θ) allows r<0 with convention: (-r, θ) = (r, θ + pi). PreCalc Regents tests; AP Calc BC: polar area A = (1/2) integral r^2 dθ (compare with parametric arc length and Cartesian area to integrate).

questions.prompt: Convert Cartesian (1, 1) to polar with r > 0 and θ in [0, 2pi).
choices: integral from a to b of sqrt((dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2) dt
questions.explanation: Cartesian to polar: r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2) = sqrt(1+1) = sqrt(2). θ = arctan(y/x) = arctan(1) = pi/4, with quadrant check: point (1,1) is in Q1, so pi/4 is correct (no adjustment needed). Polar: (sqrt(2), pi/4). Quadrant matters: arctan returns values in (-pi/2, pi/2), so for Q2 add pi, Q3 add pi, Q4 add 2pi (or subtract). For (-1, 1) in Q2: arctan(-1) = -pi/4, but actual angle is 3pi/4 (add pi). PreCalc Regents conversion item; AP Calc BC core fluency.

questions.prompt: Polar equation r = 4 in Cartesian.
questions.explanation: Convert r = 4: square both sides (since r >= 0): r^2 = 16. Use r^2 = x^2 + y^2: x^2 + y^2 = 16. This is a circle of radius 4 centered at origin. Polar makes circles centered at origin trivially expressible. Polar r = a (constant) is always such a circle. r = a sin θ or r = a cos θ are circles NOT centered at origin (they pass through origin). PreCalc Regents tests conversion. AP Calc BC: area of region in polar = (1/2) integral r^2 dθ from theta_1 to theta_2 - elegant for radial regions.

questions.prompt: Polar equation r = 2 + 2cos θ in Cartesian.
questions.explanation: Cardioid: r = 2 + 2cos θ. Multiply both sides by r: r^2 = 2r + 2r cos θ. Substitute r^2 = x^2+y^2 and r cos θ = x: x^2 + y^2 = 2r + 2x. Move 2x: x^2 + y^2 - 2x = 2r. Square: (x^2+y^2-2x)^2 = 4r^2 = 4(x^2+y^2). Messy 4th-degree equation, but polar form r=2+2cos θ is clean. This curve is a heart-shaped cardioid (named for Greek 'kardia' = heart). PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC tests area inside cardioid: A = (1/2) integral_0^(2pi) (2+2cos θ)^2 dθ = 6 pi.

questions.prompt: Equation form for parabola opening up with vertex (h, k).
questions.explanation: Parabola standard form: (x-h)^2 = 4p(y-k) opens UP if p>0, DOWN if p<0. Vertex (h,k). Focus at (h, k+p). Directrix y = k-p. Coefficient 4p controls how 'wide' the parabola is. Reflection property: rays parallel to axis reflect through focus (used in satellite dishes, headlights). For horizontal: (y-k)^2 = 4p(x-h) opens right if p>0. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc test identifying h, k, p from given equation. AP Calc applies in optics, parabolic trajectories, and parametric equations of falling objects.

questions.prompt: Locus definition of parabola.
choices: Set of points equidistant from focus and directrix
questions.correctText: Set of points equidistant from focus and directrix
questions.explanation: Parabola locus: Set of all points P such that distance from P to focus F equals distance from P to directrix d (a line). Equivalent to algebraic definition via standard form. Distance to focus is to a POINT; distance to directrix is perpendicular distance to a LINE. Application: reflective property comes from this geometric definition (light from focus reflects parallel to axis, and vice versa). PreCalc Regents introduces conics by locus definitions. AP Calc uses parabolas in projectile motion and integration applications (areas under arches, volume of revolution).

questions.prompt: Focus of x^2 = 8y.
choices: Set of points equidistant from focus and directrix
questions.explanation: Find focus: x^2 = 8y is in form x^2 = 4py with 4p = 8, so p = 2. Vertex at (0,0) (since h=k=0). Parabola opens up (p>0). Focus at (h, k+p) = (0, 0+2) = (0, 2). Directrix y = k-p = -2. PreCalc Regents tests extracting p from coefficient. AP Calc uses focal property: optical and audio applications focus rays via parabolic reflectors. The point (4, 2) is on the parabola: 4^2 = 16 = 8*2, and is equidistant from F=(0,2) (distance 4) and directrix y=-2 (distance 4). Verified.

questions.prompt: Vertex of (x-3)^2 = -4(y+1).
choices: Set of points equidistant from focus and directrix
questions.explanation: Vertex from standard form: (x-h)^2 = 4p(y-k) has vertex (h, k). Here (x-3)^2 = -4(y+1) = -4(y-(-1)), so h=3, k=-1. Vertex (3, -1). Coefficient 4p = -4 means p = -1 (negative), so parabola opens DOWN. Focus: (h, k+p) = (3, -2). Directrix: y = k-p = 0 (the x-axis). PreCalc Regents standard problem. AP Calc: shifted parabolas appear in projectile motion (vertex = peak of trajectory) and in completing the square problems for area-under-curve integrals.

questions.prompt: Convert y = x^2 + 4x + 7 to vertex form.
questions.explanation: Complete the square: y = x^2 + 4x + 7 = (x^2 + 4x + 4) + 7 - 4 = (x+2)^2 + 3. Add and subtract (b/2)^2 = (4/2)^2 = 4. Vertex form: y - 3 = (x+2)^2, so vertex (-2, 3). Comparison with (x-h)^2 = 4p(y-k): rewrite as (x+2)^2 = 1*(y-3), so 4p = 1, p = 1/4. Focus (-2, 3.25); directrix y = 2.75. PreCalc Regents: completing the square essential for identifying vertex and analyzing conics. AP Calc: same technique for integrating 1/(x^2+4x+13) via completing square inside arctan integral.

questions.prompt: Standard form for ellipse centered at origin.
questions.explanation: Ellipse standard form: x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 (center origin). a, b are semi-axes. If a > b: major axis horizontal, vertices at (+/-a, 0), minor axis vertices at (0, +/-b). If b > a: major axis vertical. Foci: at (+/-c, 0) where c^2 = a^2 - b^2 (with a > b). Eccentricity e = c/a, between 0 (circle) and 1 (parabola). PreCalc Regents identifies a, b, c from equation. AP Calc: area of ellipse = pi*a*b (generalizes pi*r^2 circle). Kepler's first law: planets orbit Sun in ellipses with Sun at focus.

questions.prompt: Eccentricity range for ellipse.
questions.explanation: Eccentricity of ellipse: e = c/a where c = sqrt(a^2 - b^2), a is semi-major axis. Range: 0 <= e < 1. e=0 is a circle (foci coincide at center). e approaching 1 means ellipse becomes elongated (foci far apart relative to a). Parabola has e=1 exactly. Hyperbola has e>1. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc: eccentricity classifies conics. Earth's orbital eccentricity ≈ 0.017 (nearly circular); Mars ≈ 0.093; comets often e > 0.9 (very elongated). Kepler's first law: orbits are ellipses with Sun at one focus.

questions.prompt: Foci of ellipse x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1.
questions.explanation: Foci of horizontal ellipse: a^2 = 25 (so a=5), b^2 = 9 (so b=3). c^2 = a^2 - b^2 = 25 - 9 = 16, c=4. Major axis is x-axis (since a > b). Foci at (+/- c, 0) = (+/- 4, 0). Vertices: (+/- 5, 0). Minor axis vertices: (0, +/- 3). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc tests foci computation. Focus-directrix property: for any point P on ellipse, |PF_1| + |PF_2| = 2a (constant sum). This is the locus definition of ellipse - 'two tacks and a string' geometric construction.

questions.prompt: Locus definition of ellipse.
choices: Sum of distances to two foci is constant
questions.correctText: Sum of distances to two foci is constant
questions.explanation: Ellipse locus: |PF_1| + |PF_2| = 2a for any point P on the ellipse, where F_1 and F_2 are the foci and 2a is the major axis length. Geometric construction: drive two thumbtacks (foci), loop a string around them, trace with pencil keeping string taut - traces an ellipse. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc apply: orbital mechanics, whispering galleries (sound from one focus reflects to other). NASA mission planning uses ellipse properties. Reflection property: rays from one focus reflect through the other (used in lithotripsy to break kidney stones).

questions.prompt: Standard form of ellipse with center (2, -1), a=4 horizontal, b=2.
questions.explanation: Shifted ellipse: General form (x-h)^2/a^2 + (y-k)^2/b^2 = 1 with center (h,k). Here center (2,-1), so h=2, k=-1. Horizontal a=4 (semi-major), so a^2=16. Vertical b=2 (semi-minor), so b^2=4. Final: (x-2)^2/16 + (y+1)^2/4 = 1. Foci at (h+/-c, k) with c=sqrt(16-4)=sqrt(12)=2sqrt(3): foci approximately (2+/-3.46, -1). PreCalc Regents tests shifted conics. AP Calc: parametric form x = h + a cos t, y = k + b sin t parameterizes shifted ellipse, useful for area integrals and arc length.

questions.prompt: Standard form for horizontal hyperbola centered at origin.
choices: Sum of distances to two foci is constant
questions.explanation: Horizontal hyperbola: x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 (center origin). Vertices at (+/-a, 0). Foci at (+/-c, 0) where c^2 = a^2 + b^2 (note PLUS for hyperbola, MINUS for ellipse). Asymptotes: y = +/-(b/a)x. The conjugate hyperbola (vertical) is y^2/b^2 - x^2/a^2 = 1 with vertices (0, +/-b). PreCalc Regents tests differences from ellipse: subtraction sign, c^2 formula, foci farther than vertices. AP Calc: hyperbolic functions cosh(t) = (e^t+e^(-t))/2 and sinh(t) parameterize hyperbola: x = cosh t, y = sinh t traces x^2 - y^2 = 1.

questions.prompt: Eccentricity range for hyperbola.
choices: Absolute difference of distances to two foci is constant
questions.explanation: Hyperbola eccentricity: e = c/a > 1 (since c > a for hyperbola, where c^2 = a^2 + b^2). The larger e, the more 'open' the hyperbola (asymptotes closer to vertical). Limiting case e -> 1+ approaches parabola; e -> inf approaches two parallel lines. Comets often have hyperbolic orbits when they pass through solar system unbounded (escape velocity). PreCalc Regents classifies conics by eccentricity: e=0 circle, 0<e<1 ellipse, e=1 parabola, e>1 hyperbola. Also Dandelin spheres geometrically show all conics as plane intersections of cone.

questions.prompt: Asymptotes of x^2/9 - y^2/16 = 1.
choices: Absolute difference of distances to two foci is constant
questions.explanation: Hyperbola asymptotes: For x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1, asymptotes are y = +/- (b/a)x. Here a^2 = 9 (a=3), b^2 = 16 (b=4), so asymptotes y = +/- (4/3)x. Comes from large-x analysis: as x->inf, (x/3)^2 dominates 1, so y^2 ≈ (16/9)x^2 (b^2/a^2 * x^2), giving y ≈ +/- (b/a)x. Method to graph: draw vertices, draw asymptote lines, sketch curves approaching asymptotes. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc tests this. End behavior of hyperbola matches asymptote behavior, similar to rational function slant asymptotes.

Foci: c^2 = a^2 + b^2 = 9 + 16 = 25, c = 5. Foci at (+/- c, 0) = (+/- 5, 0). Note for hyperbola c > a (foci OUTSIDE vertices) which differs from ellipse where c < a (foci INSIDE vertices). Vertices at (+/- 3, 0). PreCalc Regents distinguishes hyperbola vs ellipse formulas. AP Calc uses hyperbolas for inverse square laws (Coulomb, gravitation force as function of distance). Locus definition: |PF_1 - PF_2| = 2a (constant DIFFERENCE) for hyperbola, contrasted with constant SUM for ellipse.

questions.prompt: Locus definition of hyperbola.
choices: Absolute difference of distances to two foci is constant
questions.correctText: Absolute difference of distances to two foci is constant
questions.explanation: Hyperbola locus: ||PF_1| - |PF_2|| = 2a (constant) for any point on the hyperbola. Two branches: closer-to-F_1 branch has |PF_1| - |PF_2| = -2a; closer-to-F_2 branch has |PF_1| - |PF_2| = 2a. Together, absolute difference = 2a. Application: LORAN (radio navigation) - measure time difference between two signals to determine hyperbola you're on. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc tests recognizing locus definitions of all conics. Reflection property: rays from one focus reflect AWAY from other focus (used in Cassegrain telescopes).

questions.prompt: Polar curve r = a (constant).
choices: Circle of diameter a passing through origin
Circle of radius a centered at origin
questions.correctText: Circle of radius a centered at origin
questions.explanation: Polar circle: r = a (constant) is a circle of radius |a| centered at the origin. All points have same distance r from origin, so they form a circle. In Cartesian: x^2 + y^2 = a^2. Polar form is elegantly simple for radial symmetry. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC use polar for problems where Cartesian is awkward. Example: r = 5 is x^2+y^2=25; r=2 is x^2+y^2=4. AP Calc BC: area inside r=a circle = (1/2) integral_0^(2pi) a^2 dθ = pi*a^2 (matches familiar formula).

questions.prompt: Polar curve r = a sin θ (a > 0).
choices: Circle of diameter a passing through origin
questions.correctText: Circle of diameter a passing through origin
questions.explanation: Polar r = a sin θ: Multiply by r: r^2 = a r sin θ = a y (using r sin θ = y). Substitute r^2 = x^2+y^2: x^2 + y^2 = a y, completing square: x^2 + (y - a/2)^2 = (a/2)^2. Circle of radius a/2 centered at (0, a/2), passing through origin (tangent to x-axis at origin). Similarly r = a cos θ is a circle through origin tangent to y-axis. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: tracing polar curves over [0, 2pi] when a > 0 traces circle TWICE (once for r>0, once for r<0).

questions.prompt: Polar curve r = 1 + cos θ.
questions.explanation: Cardioid: r = 1 + cos θ (or any r = a(1 + cos θ) variant). Heart-shaped curve. Symmetric about x-axis (since replacing θ with -θ gives same r). r ranges from 0 (at θ = pi) to 2 (at θ = 0). Special case of limaçon r = a + b cos θ when |a| = |b|. AP Calc BC: area inside cardioid = (1/2) integral_0^(2pi) (1+cos θ)^2 dθ = 3 pi/2. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC tests area calculations. Cardioid appears in microphone directional patterns ('cardioid mic' picks up sound from one direction only).

questions.prompt: Polar curve r = 2 cos(2θ).
choices: Circle of radius 3 centered at origin
Circle of diameter a passing through origin
questions.explanation: Rose curves: r = a cos(nθ) or r = a sin(nθ). Number of petals: n if n is odd, 2n if n is even. Here n=2 (even), so 2*2 = 4 petals. Each petal traced as θ varies over interval where r >= 0. Length of each petal = |a|. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: identify petal count and area. AP Calc BC area of one petal of r = a cos(nθ) (n even, n petals total within [0, 2pi]): A = (1/2) integral (over interval where cos >= 0) of a^2 cos^2(nθ) dθ = pi*a^2/(4n) per petal.

questions.prompt: Identify the polar point equivalent to (3, pi/4) with r negative.
choices: Circle of radius a centered at origin
questions.explanation: Polar equivalence: (r, θ) = (-r, θ + pi). So (3, pi/4) = (-3, pi/4 + pi) = (-3, 5pi/4). Verify: -3 cos(5pi/4) = -3*(-sqrt(2)/2) = 3sqrt(2)/2; -3 sin(5pi/4) = -3*(-sqrt(2)/2) = 3sqrt(2)/2. Same as 3 cos(pi/4) = 3sqrt(2)/2 and 3 sin(pi/4) = 3sqrt(2)/2. Both give Cartesian (3sqrt(2)/2, 3sqrt(2)/2). PreCalc Regents tests this less commonly; AP Calc BC: matters when tracing polar curves where r changes sign (r = 1 - 2cos θ has loops because r goes negative).

questions.prompt: Polar coordinates of a point.
questions.explanation: (r, θ) where r is distance from origin (the 'pole') and θ is angle from positive x-axis (the 'polar axis'). Convention: θ measured counterclockwise. Multiple representations: (r, θ) = (r, θ + 2pi n) for any integer n; also (-r, θ) = (r, θ + pi) using negative r convention. Origin has any θ (special case). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: many problems begin 'convert (r, θ) =... to Cartesian.' Polar excels for radial symmetry, rotation problems, spirals. Calc III extends to cylindrical and spherical coordinates.

questions.prompt: Cartesian formula for r given (x, y).
questions.explanation: Find r: r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2). Distance formula from origin. Always non-negative. Examples: point (3,4): r = sqrt(9+16) = 5. Point (-1, 1): r = sqrt(1+1) = sqrt(2). Note r is uniquely positive in standard polar; θ may have multiple representations modulo 2pi. PreCalc Regents tests conversion. AP Calc BC and Calc III use polar substitution for integrals over circular regions: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ, dx dy = r dr dθ (Jacobian gives extra factor of r). Integral over disk x^2+y^2 <= R^2 becomes 0 to R for r, 0 to 2pi for θ.

questions.prompt: Express line y = x in polar form.
questions.explanation: Polar line through origin: y = x means tan θ = y/x = 1, so θ = pi/4 (in Q1) or θ = pi/4 + pi = 5pi/4 (in Q3, when r > 0 still gives points on line). Polar equation: θ = pi/4 (with r varying over all reals, including negatives, traces the whole line) or use both θ values with r >= 0. PreCalc Regents tests; AP Calc BC: line through origin in polar is θ = constant. Generalizes to y = mx as θ = arctan(m). For lines NOT through origin, polar form is r = d sec(θ - α) as in the previous category.

questions.prompt: Polar equation of a line that does NOT pass through origin.
questions.explanation: Polar line: A line at perpendicular distance d from origin, with the perpendicular making angle α with x-axis, has polar equation r cos(θ - α) = d, equivalently r = d/cos(θ - α) = d sec(θ - α). The foot of perpendicular is at (d cos α, d sin α). Vertical line x = c is r cos θ = c, so r = c sec θ. Horizontal line y = c is r sin θ = c, so r = c csc θ. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: polar equation of common geometric figures. Lines through origin: θ = α (constant, just rotate radially).

questions.prompt: Polar coordinates of Cartesian (-1, -sqrt(3)) with r > 0 and θ in [0, 2pi).
questions.explanation: Cartesian to polar: (-1, -sqrt(3)). r = sqrt(1 + 3) = sqrt(4) = 2. arctan(y/x) = arctan(-sqrt(3)/(-1)) = arctan(sqrt(3)) = pi/3. But this is the REFERENCE angle; quadrant is Q3 (both x and y negative). In Q3, θ = pi + pi/3 = 4pi/3. Verify: 2 cos(4pi/3) = 2*(-1/2) = -1; 2 sin(4pi/3) = 2*(-sqrt(3)/2) = -sqrt(3). PreCalc Regents tests quadrant-aware conversion. AP Calc BC: critical for setting up polar integrals where bounds depend on starting angle.

questions.prompt: Limit notation: lim_(x->a) f(x) = L means.
choices: f(x) approaches L as x approaches a
questions.correctText: f(x) approaches L as x approaches a
questions.explanation: Limit definition (informal): lim x->a f(x) = L means that f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L by taking x sufficiently close to a (but not equal to a). Formal epsilon-delta: for every epsilon > 0, exists delta > 0 such that 0 < |x - a| < delta implies |f(x) - L| < epsilon (Cauchy 1820s, refined by Weierstrass). PreCalc Regents introduces conceptually; AP Calc BC works at level of intuition and direct computation. Critical: x APPROACHES a, doesn't EQUAL a; so f(a) need not exist for the limit to exist.

questions.prompt: Type of sequence with common difference between consecutive terms.
questions.explanation: Arithmetic sequence: a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d where d is the common difference. Each term differs from previous by exactly d. Examples: 5, 8, 11, 14,... (d=3). nth term formula essential for finding any term without listing all. Sum of first n terms: S_n = n(a_1 + a_n)/2 = n[2a_1 + (n-1)d]/2. PreCalc Regents and Algebra 2 Regents standard. AP Calc BC: arithmetic series diverge (unless d=0 and a_1=0), since terms don't approach 0. Gauss as a child famously computed 1+2+...+100 = 5050 = 100*101/2.

questions.prompt: 10th term of geometric sequence 2, 6, 18,....
questions.explanation: Geometric sequence: a_n = a_1 * r^(n-1). Common ratio r = 6/2 = 3. First term a_1 = 2. a_10 = 2 * 3^(10-1) = 2 * 3^9 = 2 * 19683 = 39366. PreCalc Regents and Algebra 2 tests this formula. Geometric sequences grow exponentially - much faster than arithmetic. AP Calc BC: geometric SERIES (sum) converges iff |r| < 1, with sum a/(1-r). Famous geometric series: 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 +... = 1 (Zeno's paradox resolved).

questions.prompt: Sigma notation for 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25.
choices: f(x) approaches L as x approaches a
questions.explanation: Sigma notation: Σ from n=1 to 5 of n^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 = 55. n is dummy index; can be i, k, j etc. Bounds 1 to 5 inclusive. Useful closed forms: Σ_{i=1}^n 1 = n; Σ_{i=1}^n i = n(n+1)/2; Σ_{i=1}^n i^2 = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6; Σ_{i=1}^n i^3 = [n(n+1)/2]^2. PreCalc Regents introduces sigma notation. AP Calc BC: definite integral defined as limit of Riemann sums in sigma notation: integral_a^b f(x) dx = lim n->inf Σ f(x_i)Δx.

questions.prompt: L'Hopital's Rule says lim f/g when 0/0 form equals.
choices: f(x) approaches L as x approaches a
f(a) defined, lim exists, lim = f(a)
questions.explanation: L'Hopital's Rule: For 0/0 or inf/inf indeterminate forms, lim x->a f(x)/g(x) = lim x->a f'(x)/g'(x) (if RHS limit exists). Iterate if still indeterminate. Bernoulli 1696 (published by L'Hopital). Examples: lim x->0 sin x/x: f'=cos x, g'=1, ratio at 0 is 1/1 = 1. lim x->inf x/e^x: ratio x/e^x is inf/inf, derivatives 1/e^x -> 0 as x -> inf. AP Calc BC heavily tests L'Hopital. NOTE: only applies to 0/0 and inf/inf; other indeterminate forms (0*inf, inf-inf, 0^0, 1^inf, inf^0) require algebraic manipulation first.

Sum 1 to n: 1 + 2 +... + n = n(n+1)/2 (Gauss's formula, allegedly derived as a child). For n=100: 100*101/2 = 5050. Pairing trick: 1+100 = 101, 2+99 = 101,..., 50+51 = 101; 50 pairs of 101 = 5050. General arithmetic series: S = n(first + last)/2. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC use closed-form summations to evaluate Riemann sums in calculus (sum of i, i^2, i^3 over i=1 to n have known formulas critical for FTC proof).

questions.prompt: Sum of geometric series 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 +....
questions.explanation: Infinite geometric series: 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 +... = a/(1-r) where a is first term and r is common ratio. Here a=1, r=1/2: sum = 1/(1-1/2) = 1/(1/2) = 2. Converges iff |r| < 1; here |1/2| < 1, OK. Famous resolves Zeno's paradox: infinite distances add to finite total. AP Calc BC heavily tests geometric series, often with r in terms of x: sum x^n = 1/(1-x) for |x|<1 (geometric series in disguise). Derivative and integral give Taylor series of ln(1-x) and 1/(1-x)^2.

questions.prompt: Type of sequence each term defined using previous term(s).
choices: The exponent such that b^y = x
questions.explanation: Recursive sequence: Each term depends on one or more previous terms. Example: Fibonacci F_n = F_(n-1) + F_(n-2) with F_1=F_2=1, generating 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21,... Both an explicit formula (closed-form, like a_n = 2n+1) and recursive can describe same sequence; converting between can be challenging. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC and Calc III use recursion for Taylor series coefficients and integration by parts repeatedly. Recursive Fibonacci has closed form: F_n = (phi^n - psi^n)/sqrt(5) (Binet's formula, phi = golden ratio).

questions.prompt: First term that diverges: harmonic series sum 1/n.
questions.explanation: Harmonic series: Σ 1/n = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 +... DIVERGES, even though terms approach 0. Proof (Oresme, 1350): group as 1 + (1/2) + (1/3+1/4) + (1/5+1/6+1/7+1/8) +... where each group sums to >= 1/2; infinitely many groups means sum diverges. Famous counterexample showing 'terms going to 0' is necessary but NOT sufficient for convergence. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC: harmonic series is borderline divergent; Σ 1/n^p converges iff p > 1 (p-series test). Σ 1/n^2 = pi^2/6 (Basel problem, Euler).

Leibniz series: 1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7 +... = pi/4 (Leibniz 1676; James Gregory had it earlier). Derives from Taylor series arctan(x) = x - x^3/3 + x^5/5 -... evaluated at x=1. Converges to pi/4 but VERY slowly (millions of terms needed for 6 decimal places of pi). AP Calc BC: Maclaurin series for arctan, derive Leibniz formula at x=1. Faster convergence: Machin's formula pi/4 = 4 arctan(1/5) - arctan(1/239) used for computing pi to many decimal places before computers.

questions.prompt: Compute lim_(x->0^+) 1/x.
questions.explanation: One-sided limit at vertical asymptote: lim x->0^+ 1/x = +inf. As x approaches 0 from the right (positive small numbers), 1/x grows without bound positively. From the left: lim x->0^- 1/x = -inf. Two-sided limit doesn't exist. Vertical asymptote x=0. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: one-sided limits at VAs reveal whether function approaches +inf or -inf, important for graphing and improper integrals. Improper integral integral_0^1 1/x dx is integral with infinite value at x=0, requiring lim approach.

questions.prompt: Compute lim_(x->infinity) (5x^2 + 3)/(2x^2 - 7).
questions.explanation: Limit at infinity for rational: same degree numerator and denominator, ratio of leading coefficients. Here 5/2 = 2.5. Verify: divide num and denom by x^2: (5 + 3/x^2)/(2 - 7/x^2) -> 5/2 as x -> inf. PreCalc Regents: horizontal asymptote of rational functions. AP Calc BC: limits at infinity for rational functions of x have horizontal asymptotes corresponding to these ratios. End behavior of polynomials: lim x->inf of ax^n + lower order = sign(a)*inf if n>0. Rational comparison: degree determines limit.

questions.prompt: Limit lim_(h->0) (sin h)/h equals.
questions.explanation: Famous limit: lim h->0 (sin h)/h = 1 (h in radians). Foundation for derivative of sin: d/dx[sin x] = cos x derived from this limit. Geometric proof: sandwich theorem with cos h < (sin h)/h < 1 for small positive h, both bounds approach 1. WARNING: only works in radians; degrees give limit pi/180. Related: lim h->0 (1 - cos h)/h = 0; lim h->0 (1 - cos h)/h^2 = 1/2. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc proves rigorously. L'Hopital's rule shortcut: 0/0 form, derivative of top is cos h, of bottom is 1; ratio at h=0 is 1.

questions.prompt: Limit of a_n = (3n+1)/(n+2) as n approaches infinity.
questions.explanation: Limit of rational sequence: a_n = (3n+1)/(n+2). Divide num/denom by n: a_n = (3 + 1/n)/(1 + 2/n). As n -> inf: numerator -> 3+0=3, denominator -> 1+0=1, so a_n -> 3. Rule: limit of rational expression in n equals ratio of leading coefficients when num/denom degrees match. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc fundamental for limits at infinity. Convergent sequence: limit exists and is finite. Divergent sequence: limit doesn't exist or is infinite. AP Calc BC uses limits to determine series convergence.

questions.prompt: Determine lim_(x->infinity) (x^3 + 2x)/(e^x).
questions.explanation: Polynomial vs exponential: lim x->inf (x^3+2x)/e^x. e^x grows faster than any polynomial, so limit is 0. Verify via L'Hopital (3 times): (x^3+2x)/e^x -> (3x^2+2)/e^x -> 6x/e^x -> 6/e^x -> 0. Each iteration uses inf/inf form. Hierarchy: ln(x) < x^p < e^x < x^x as x -> inf. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC tests these limits routinely. Application: probability density functions like Gaussian e^(-x^2) decay faster than any polynomial - moments E[X^n] all finite.

questions.prompt: Sequence a_n = 2n + 1 has first three terms.
choices: x -> infinity: y -> infinity; x -> -infinity: y -> -infinity
questions.explanation: Evaluate sequence: a_n = 2n+1 (assume index starts at n=1). a_1 = 2(1)+1 = 3; a_2 = 2(2)+1 = 5; a_3 = 2(3)+1 = 7. Pattern: odd numbers starting at 3, common difference 2. This is an arithmetic sequence. Notation varies: some start at n=0 (giving 1, 3, 5,...). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC test sequence evaluation. AP Calc BC: series sum_n a_n where a_n is the nth term; convergence depends on behavior of a_n as n -> infinity.

questions.prompt: End behavior of f(x) = x^3.
choices: x -> infinity: y -> infinity; x -> -infinity: y -> -infinity
questions.correctText: x -> infinity: y -> infinity; x -> -infinity: y -> -infinity
questions.explanation: End behavior of x^3: As x -> +inf, x^3 -> +inf. As x -> -inf, x^3 -> -inf. Cubic function: odd-degree, positive leading coefficient, so behavior is 'down on left, up on right' (opposite ends). PreCalc Regents: leading term determines end behavior. General: a_n x^n with n odd and a_n > 0: down-up; n odd, a_n < 0: up-down; n even, a_n > 0: up-up; n even, a_n < 0: down-down. AP Calc uses end behavior for horizontal asymptotes and for determining whether integrals converge at infinity (improper integrals).

questions.prompt: End behavior of e^x as x -> infinity.
choices: x -> infinity: y -> infinity; x -> -infinity: y -> -infinity
Circle of radius a centered at origin
questions.explanation: Exponential growth: lim x->inf e^x = +inf. Grows faster than any polynomial: lim x->inf x^n/e^x = 0 for any positive n (provable by L'Hopital n times). As x -> -inf, e^x -> 0+ (horizontal asymptote y=0 from above). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc test this. Comparison: exponentials beat polynomials (long-term); polynomials beat logarithms (long-term). Hierarchy: ln x < x^p (for any p > 0) < e^x as x -> inf. AP Calc BC: series convergence comparisons use this hierarchy heavily.

questions.prompt: End behavior of ln(x) as x -> infinity.
choices: The exponent such that b^y = x
x -> infinity: y -> infinity; x -> -infinity: y -> -infinity
questions.explanation: Logarithmic growth: lim x->inf ln x = +inf, but very slowly. ln(10) ≈ 2.3, ln(100) ≈ 4.6, ln(10^6) ≈ 13.8. Grows slower than any positive power of x: lim x->inf ln x / x^p = 0 for any p > 0. As x -> 0+, ln x -> -inf (vertical asymptote at x=0). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc: ln is the slowest-growing common function. AP Calc BC: ln(n) appears in integral test for series: Σ 1/(n ln n) diverges but Σ 1/(n (ln n)^2) converges (subtle borderline).

questions.prompt: End behavior of f(x) = (2x+3)/(x-1) as x -> infinity.
choices: x -> infinity: y -> infinity; x -> -infinity: y -> -infinity
questions.explanation: Limit at infinity for rational: same degree (both linear), so limit = ratio of leading coefficients = 2/1 = 2. Horizontal asymptote y = 2 (both as x -> +inf and x -> -inf). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc: identify HA from rational function. Verify: divide num and denom by x: (2 + 3/x)/(1 - 1/x) -> 2/1 = 2. AP Calc BC: limits at infinity for rational functions tested constantly; combine with one-sided limits to fully characterize a rational function's graph.

questions.prompt: Notation for left-hand limit.
questions.explanation: Left-hand limit: lim x->a^- f(x), x approaching a from values less than a (the left side). Symbol: minus sign in superscript. Right-hand limit: lim x->a^+ f(x) (plus sign), approaching from above. Two-sided limit exists iff both one-sided limits exist AND are equal. Example: f(x) = |x|/x has lim x->0^- = -1 and lim x->0^+ = 1, so two-sided limit doesn't exist (jump discontinuity). PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC tests one-sided limits at piecewise function junctions and at vertical asymptotes.

questions.prompt: Compute lim_(x->2) (x^2 - 4)/(x - 2).
questions.explanation: Indeterminate 0/0: Direct substitution gives 0/0. Factor: (x^2-4)/(x-2) = (x-2)(x+2)/(x-2) = x+2 for x ≠ 2. Limit as x->2: 2+2 = 4. Removable discontinuity (hole at x=2, value 4). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc fundamental technique: algebraic simplification before substitution. AP Calc BC: this anticipates derivative definition f'(a) = lim h->0 (f(a+h)-f(a))/h, which is always a 0/0 indeterminate that requires algebra (or L'Hopital) to evaluate.

questions.prompt: F is continuous at x = a if these three conditions hold.
choices: f(a) defined, lim exists, lim = f(a)
x = t - sin t, y = 1 - cos t
questions.correctText: f(a) defined, lim exists, lim = f(a)
questions.explanation: Continuity at x=a: three conditions must ALL hold: (1) f(a) is defined; (2) lim x->a f(x) exists (both one-sided limits exist and are equal); (3) lim x->a f(x) = f(a). Failure of any one = discontinuity. Types of discontinuity: removable (hole; lim exists but f(a) different or undefined), jump (one-sided limits differ), infinite (one-sided limit is +/-inf), oscillating (no limit exists). PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC tests with piecewise functions and rational functions. IVT requires continuity on [a,b].

questions.prompt: Type of discontinuity at x=0 for f(x) = |x|/x.
choices: f(a) defined, lim exists, lim = f(a)
questions.explanation: Jump discontinuity: One-sided limits exist but are different. f(x) = |x|/x = -1 for x<0, +1 for x>0; lim x->0^- = -1, lim x->0^+ = +1. The function 'jumps' between -1 and +1 at x=0. Not removable - can't fix by redefining f(0). Other discontinuity types: removable (lim exists, just f(a) wrong), infinite (one-sided limit -> +/-inf, e.g., 1/x at x=0), oscillating (e.g., sin(1/x) at x=0). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: classification of discontinuities is foundational, with each type behaving differently in IVT and FTC applications.

questions.prompt: Find c so f piecewise (x^2 for x<2; 3x+c for x>=2) is continuous at x=2.
choices: f(a) defined, lim exists, lim = f(a)
f must be continuous on the closed interval [a,b]
questions.explanation: Find c for continuity: f(x) = x^2 for x<2 has lim x->2^- = 2^2 = 4. f(x) = 3x+c for x>=2 has value at x=2: 3*2+c = 6+c. For continuity, need 6+c = 4, so c = -2. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: piecewise continuity problems. Also matches limit from right: lim x->2^+ (3x+c) = 6+c = 4 by continuity. So function is continuous if c=-2. AP Calc BC extends to differentiability: requiring same DERIVATIVE at junction adds another condition. f differentiable => continuous (contrapositive: discontinuous => not differentiable).

questions.prompt: Inverse of f(x) = e^x.
questions.explanation: Inverse: e^x and ln(x) are mutual inverses. f(f^-1(x)) = e^(ln x) = x (for x>0); f^-1(f(x)) = ln(e^x) = x (for all real x). Graphs are reflections across y=x. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc test this. Practical use: solve exp equations by taking ln; solve log equations by exponentiating. Properties: ln(e) = 1, e^(ln a) = a, ln(a^b) = b ln a. AP Calc BC: d/dx[e^x] = e^x; d/dx[ln x] = 1/x; integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C.

questions.prompt: Maximum number of real roots of a degree 4 polynomial.
choices: x-intercepts at x=1 and x=-3; vertical asymptote at x=2; slant asymptote y=x+2
questions.explanation: Degree 4 polynomial has exactly 4 complex roots (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra), of which at most 4 are real (with 0, 1, 2, 3, or 4 real depending on discriminant and structure). Possible splittings: 4 real; 2 real + 1 conjugate pair; 0 real + 2 conjugate pairs. Example: x^4 - 10 x^2 + 9 = (x^2-1)(x^2-9) has 4 real roots +/-1, +/-3. x^4+1 has 0 real roots, 2 conjugate pairs. PreCalc Regents tests this. AP Calc: end behavior of degree 4 (even, positive leading) is up-up; bisection method finds real roots one at a time using IVT.

questions.prompt: Continuity of f(x) = sin(x) for all x.
questions.explanation: Sin x is continuous everywhere: defined for all real x, limit exists at every point and equals function value. Same for cos x. Tan x is NOT continuous everywhere - has VAs at pi/2 + n*pi (where cos=0). PreCalc Regents and AP Calc: sin and cos are 'nice' (continuous, differentiable, bounded, periodic). AP Calc: continuous functions on closed intervals have absolute max/min (Extreme Value Theorem). sin and cos are integrable on any interval. d/dx[sin x] = cos x; integral of sin x dx = -cos x + C.

questions.prompt: Solve 2 cos x = 1 in [0, 2pi).
questions.explanation: Solve 2 cos x = 1: cos x = 1/2. Reference angle arccos(1/2) = pi/3 (60°). Cosine is positive in Q1 and Q4: solutions pi/3 (Q1) and 2pi - pi/3 = 5pi/3 (Q4). Verify: cos(pi/3) = 1/2 OK; cos(5pi/3) = cos(-pi/3) = 1/2 OK. PreCalc Regents standard. AP Calc: similar trig equations arise in finding critical points of trig functions, e.g., critical points of f(x) = x - sin x have f'(x) = 1 - cos x = 0, so cos x = 1, giving x = 2k pi.

questions.prompt: Lim_(x -> 0) (sin 3x)/x value.
choices: x must be in domain of g and g(x) must be in domain of f
questions.explanation: Use famous limit: lim x->0 sin(x)/x = 1. Rewrite: lim x->0 sin(3x)/x = lim x->0 3 * sin(3x)/(3x) = 3 * 1 = 3. Or L'Hopital: derivative of sin(3x) is 3 cos(3x), derivative of x is 1, ratio at x=0 is 3*1/1 = 3. General pattern: lim x->0 sin(kx)/x = k. PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc tests routinely. Variants: lim x->0 tan(kx)/x = k; lim x->0 (1-cos kx)/x^2 = k^2/2. All foundational for derivative formulas of trig functions.

questions.prompt: Pythagorean identity equivalent for sec.
questions.explanation: Pythagorean identity: 1 + tan^2 x = sec^2 x. Derives from sin^2+cos^2=1 divided by cos^2. AP Calc BC trig substitution: integral with sqrt(a^2 + x^2) form -> let x = a tan θ, then a^2 + x^2 = a^2 sec^2 θ, and dx = a sec^2 θ dθ; simplifies to sec^3 θ or other integrable forms. PreCalc Regents: applies in identity proofs and simplifications. Memorize all three Pythagorean: sin^2+cos^2=1, tan^2+1=sec^2, 1+cot^2=csc^2.

questions.prompt: Period of f(x) = tan(2x).
questions.explanation: Period of tan(Bx): pi/|B|. Tan has natural period pi (half of sin and cos's 2pi). Here B=2: period = pi/2. So tan(2x) completes one full cycle every pi/2 units. Asymptotes: 2x = pi/2 + n*pi gives x = pi/4 + n*pi/2. PreCalc Regents tests transformations of tan. AP Calc BC: tan in integrals via tan substitution. d/dx[tan x] = sec^2 x; integral of tan x dx = -ln|cos x| + C = ln|sec x| + C.

questions.prompt: X-coordinate of vertex of parabola y = 3x^2 - 12x + 7.
choices: Reflection over the line y = x
questions.explanation: Vertex of parabola y = ax^2 + bx + c: x = -b/(2a). Here a=3, b=-12: x = 12/6 = 2. Then y = 3(4) - 24 + 7 = -5. Vertex at (2, -5). Alternative: complete the square y = 3(x^2 - 4x) + 7 = 3(x-2)^2 - 12 + 7 = 3(x-2)^2 - 5. Same answer. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc test vertex location and optimization. AP Calc: vertex is critical point where derivative = 0: dy/dx = 6x - 12 = 0 at x = 2 (same answer).

questions.prompt: Solve x^2 - 4x + 13 = 0.
questions.explanation: Quadratic formula: x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2-4ac))/(2a) = (4 +/- sqrt(16-52))/2 = (4 +/- sqrt(-36))/2 = (4 +/- 6i)/2 = 2 +/- 3i. Discriminant b^2-4ac = -36 < 0, so complex conjugate pair of roots. PreCalc Regents and Algebra 2 test complex roots. AP Calc: avoids complex numbers in undergraduate calculus, but they reappear in differential equations (characteristic equation r^2 - 4r + 13 = 0 has roots 2+/-3i, giving solutions y = e^(2x)(cos 3x + i sin 3x) which expand to real form e^(2x)cos(3x), e^(2x)sin(3x)).

questions.prompt: Express sin(2x) using double-angle formula.
questions.explanation: Sin(2x) = 2 sin x cos x: From sum formula sin(x+x) = sin x cos x + cos x sin x = 2 sin x cos x. PreCalc Regents drills this; AP Calc BC tests in integration: integral of sin(2x) dx = -cos(2x)/2 + C; integral of sin x cos x dx = sin^2 x / 2 + C (chain rule) or -cos(2x)/4 + C (double-angle substitution). Also derives power-reduction: sin^2 x = (1-cos 2x)/2 useful for integrating sin^2.

questions.prompt: Magnitude of vector <6, 8>.
questions.explanation: Magnitude: sqrt(36+64) = sqrt(100) = 10. Pythagorean triple (6, 8, 10) - scaled (3, 4, 5). Unit vector: <6/10, 8/10> = <0.6, 0.8>. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC: |v| applies in physics (speed = |velocity|, distance traveled from displacement |Δr|) and geometry (length of arrow). Calc III extends to 3D: |<a, b, c>| = sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2). |v + w| <= |v| + |w| (triangle inequality). Vector dot product u. v = |u||v| cos θ relates magnitudes and angle.

questions.prompt: Standard form for ellipse x^2/16 + y^2/9 = 1 - find foci.
questions.explanation: Foci of ellipse: a^2=16 (a=4), b^2=9 (b=3), c^2 = a^2 - b^2 = 16-9 = 7, c = sqrt(7). Major axis horizontal (a>b). Foci at (+/- c, 0) = (+/- sqrt(7), 0). PreCalc Regents standard problem. Verify: |PF_1| + |PF_2| = 2a = 8 for any point P on ellipse. Eccentricity e = c/a = sqrt(7)/4 ≈ 0.661. AP Calc BC: parametric form x = 4 cos t, y = 3 sin t parameterizes ellipse for arc length and area integrals.

questions.prompt: Cardioid r = 1 + cos θ has maximum r at this angle.
choices: sin A cos B + cos A sin B
questions.explanation: Maximum of cardioid: r = 1 + cos θ. cos θ is maximized at θ=0 with cos(0)=1, giving r=1+1=2. Minimum at θ=pi: r=1-1=0 (curve passes through origin). Symmetric about x-axis. AP Calc BC: area inside cardioid = (1/2) integral_0^(2pi) (1+cos θ)^2 dθ = (1/2) integral (1 + 2 cos θ + cos^2 θ) dθ = (1/2)(2pi + 0 + pi) = 3 pi/2. PreCalc Regents introduces cardioid; AP Calc BC tests area and tangent line at specific points.

questions.prompt: Vertex of (y-2)^2 = 8(x-1).
questions.explanation: Sideways parabola: (y-k)^2 = 4p(x-h) has vertex (h,k). Here (y-2)^2 = 8(x-1) so vertex (1, 2). 4p = 8 means p = 2. Parabola opens RIGHT (p>0). Focus at (h+p, k) = (3, 2). Directrix x = h-p = -1. PreCalc Regents identifies vertex and orientation. AP Calc: dy/dx for sideways parabola requires implicit differentiation: 2(y-2)(dy/dx) = 8, so dy/dx = 4/(y-2) (undefined at vertex y=2, where vertical tangent).

questions.prompt: Parametric equations x = t^2, y = t^3 in Cartesian.
questions.explanation: Eliminate t: x = t^2 means t = +/- sqrt(x) (for x >= 0). y = t^3 = (+/- sqrt(x))^3 = +/- x^(3/2). Square: y^2 = x^3. Semicubical parabola (Neil's parabola), the first algebraic curve to have its arc length computed (Neil, 1657). Cusp at origin (where parametric path has dx/dt = 2t = 0 and dy/dt = 3t^2 = 0). PreCalc Regents introduces parametric elimination; AP Calc BC: arc length integral integral sqrt(4t^2 + 9t^4) dt = integral t sqrt(4 + 9 t^2) dt (using t > 0).

questions.prompt: Sin(pi/2) value.
questions.explanation: Sin(pi/2) = 1: At pi/2 (90°), the y-coordinate on the unit circle is at its maximum, 1. Recall unit circle points: (cos x, sin x). At pi/2: (0, 1). So sin(pi/2) = 1, cos(pi/2) = 0. Period 2pi. Maximum of sin: 1. Minimum: -1. PreCalc Regents foundational. AP Calc uses for evaluating definite integrals and as boundary value in trigonometric series. sin(pi/2 - x) = cos(x) (cofunction identity). sin(pi/2 + x) = cos(x) also (using sum formula sin(pi/2)cos(x) + cos(pi/2)sin(x) = cos x).

questions.prompt: E^(ln 7) equals.
questions.explanation: Inverse operations: e^(ln x) = x for x > 0; ln(e^x) = x for all x. Mutually inverse. So e^(ln 7) = 7 directly. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc fundamental. Applications: solve exponential and log equations using these inverses. d/dx[e^(f(x))] = e^(f(x)) * f'(x) (chain rule). Integral of e^x dx = e^x + C. Integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C - one of the most-used antiderivatives in AP Calc.

questions.prompt: Sum of infinite geometric series with a=8 and r=1/2.
questions.explanation: Infinite geometric sum: S = a/(1-r) for |r|<1. Here a=8, r=1/2: S = 8/(1-1/2) = 8/(1/2) = 16. Verify partial sums: 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 + 0.5 +... approaches 16. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc BC use geometric series for compound interest, Zeno-style problems, and as building block for Taylor series. Power series Σ x^n = 1/(1-x) for |x|<1 is geometric series with r=x. Derivatives and integrals of this series generate Taylor series for related functions.

questions.prompt: Sum from n=0 to infinity of 1/n! equals.
choices: Shift left 3, vertical stretch by 2 with reflection over x-axis, shift down 1
questions.explanation: Famous series: Σ_(n=0)^inf 1/n! = e = 2.71828182... (e^1 with Maclaurin series). General: e^x = Σ x^n/n! for all real x. AP Calc BC: this is the Taylor series for e^x centered at 0; converges for all x. Used to compute e to many decimals very efficiently (since n! grows fast). 1 + 1 + 1/2 + 1/6 + 1/24 + 1/120 + 1/720 = 2.71806 ≈ e. PreCalc Regents introduces e via continuous compounding; AP Calc BC derives via Taylor series.

questions.prompt: Constant e defined by which limit involving (1+1/n)^n.
questions.explanation: Definition of e: e = lim n->inf (1 + 1/n)^n ≈ 2.71828... (Euler, 1727). Generalization: lim n->inf (1 + x/n)^n = e^x. Application: continuous compound interest A = lim n->inf P(1 + r/n)^(nt) = P e^(rt). PreCalc Regents introduces e; AP Calc proves equivalence to series definition Σ 1/n!. Both definitions yield same value. e is also defined as unique base where d/dx[e^x] = e^x. Three equivalent definitions of e: limit, series, and derivative property.

questions.prompt: Domain of f(x) = ln(x).
questions.explanation: Domain of ln: x > 0 (strictly positive). At x=0 the natural log is undefined; for negative x it's undefined in real numbers (complex values would be ln|x| + i*pi for negative x). Range of ln: all reals. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc fundamental. Contrast: domain of sqrt(x) is x >= 0 (zero allowed for sqrt). Consistent rule: for f(x) = ln(g(x)), require g(x) > 0; for f(x) = sqrt(g(x)), require g(x) >= 0. AP Calc BC: improper integrals like integral 0 to 1 of ln(x) dx involve limits as the lower bound approaches 0.

Indeterminate 0/0: factor (x^2-25)/(x-5) = (x-5)(x+5)/(x-5) = x+5 for x ≠ 5. Limit = 5+5 = 10. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc fundamental. Removable discontinuity at x=5 (hole at point (5, 10)). This is also the slope of secant line approaching tangent line at x=5 for f(x) = x^2; equivalently f'(5) = 2x|_(x=5) = 10. AP Calc: derivative definition is exactly this type of limit, generalized.

questions.prompt: Lim_(x -> infinity) (x+1)/x.
questions.explanation: Limit at infinity: (x+1)/x = 1 + 1/x -> 1 + 0 = 1 as x -> inf. Same as ratio of leading coefficients (both linear with coefficient 1). Horizontal asymptote y=1. PreCalc Regents and AP Calc: horizontal asymptotes of rational functions. Generalizes: lim x->inf (ax+b)/(cx+d) = a/c (when c ≠ 0). AP Calc BC: limits like this appear in convergence analysis of series; e.g., ratio test lim |a_(n+1)/a_n| has rational forms.

questions.prompt: Solve log_2(x+1) = 3.
questions.explanation: Solve log equation: log_2(x+1) = 3 means 2^3 = x+1, so x+1 = 8, x = 7. Check domain: x+1 = 8 > 0 OK. PreCalc Regents standard. AP Calc: similar log equations from differential equation solutions where ln(y) = expression in x. e^(ln expression) is taken to solve for y. Example: separable ODE dy/dx = y gives ln|y| = x + C, then |y| = e^(x+C) = A e^x, so y = +/- A e^x = C e^x (after absorbing sign and constant).

questions.prompt: Continuous compound interest formula.
questions.explanation: Continuous compounding: A = P e^(rt). P = principal, r = annual rate (decimal), t = time in years. A = amount. Discrete compounding n times per year: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt); limit as n -> infinity is A = P e^(rt). PreCalc Regents introduces; AP Calc BC tests when finding time, rate, or principal. Example: $1000 at 5% continuous, double when 2P = P e^(0.05 t), so t = ln(2)/0.05 ≈ 13.86 years. Rule of 72 approximation: doubling time ≈ 72/(rate%) ≈ 14.4 years (close).
